You are working on the Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime repository.
The codebase is at version 0.6 (commit: e721269c).

## Issue to Resolve

API reference improvements
It would be nice to systematically and extensively improve our API reference:

For every API endpoint, it would be nice to provide the following (example is the place where the property does not hold):

* Extensively document what the entity represents, whether it follows ISO-8601, **how the entity can be acquired**, and what time scale it comes from. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-day-of-week/) 
* Provide a minimum of how the entity can be used. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-fixed-offset-time-zone/)
* Thoroughly explain the allowed and expected contract and behaviours and system-specifics. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-clock/)
* If applicable, provide contexts in which the entity is expected/recommended to be used (e.g. our `README` shines at that) and how it interacts with other (core) entities. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-local-date-time/)
* Provide a basic usage example. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-time-zone/)
* If applicable, provide details about conversion rules, gotchas and recommendations. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-instant/)
* Where appropriate, information about conversions and interactions with native types (e.g. Instant <-> Java Instant conversion)
* Ensure that information used in our `README` is, in fact, deducible from the core entities documentation

## Additional Context from Issue Discussion

> If applicable, provide details about conversion rules, gotchas and recommendations. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-instant/)

I don't know of gotchas related to `Instant`. The only one that comes to mind is that `instant + 1.days` is a non-calendar operation (this could be worth documenting in the `plus` function), but I'd argue it's `Duration`'s gotcha. `Instant` seems tough to use incorrectly to me. What gotchas did you have in mind?

> Where appropriate, information about conversions and interactions with native types (e.g. Instant <-> Java Instant conversion)

When you have specific behavior on some platforms, you document it in the common code, makes sense; but when you have some extra interactions with platform code, you can't document them in common code (because symbols aren't resolved). If you have platform-specific documentation, in the IDE, it is either ignored (when writing common code) or replaces the common documentation. In the latter case, you have to repeat the common documentation for platforms *and* include platform-specific additions, which I think just isn't worth it.

If the system was a bit different and platform-specific documentation was *added* to the common documentation (and maybe the IDE would be smart enough to show the documentation for the platforms the project actually uses), then I agree that mentioning platform-specific converters and so on would be beneficial. Or better yet, if there was "extension documentation" along with extension functions: then we'd be able to show `NSDate` conversions in the `Instant` documentation for Darwin targets.

## Relevant Files

The following files are likely relevant to solving this issue:
- `README.md`
- `core/build.gradle.kts`
- `core/common/src/Clock.kt`
- `core/common/src/DateTimePeriod.kt`
- `core/common/src/DateTimeUnit.kt`
- `core/common/src/DayOfWeek.kt`
- `core/common/src/Exceptions.kt`
- `core/common/src/Instant.kt`
- `core/common/src/LocalDate.kt`
- `core/common/src/LocalDateTime.kt`
- `core/common/src/LocalTime.kt`
- `core/common/src/Month.kt`
- `core/common/src/TimeZone.kt`
- `core/common/src/UtcOffset.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/DateTimeComponents.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/DateTimeFormat.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/DateTimeFormatBuilder.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/LocalDateFormat.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/Unicode.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/DateTimePeriodSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/InstantSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/LocalDateSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/LocalDateTimeSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/LocalTimeSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/TimeZoneSerializers.kt`
- `core/commonJs/src/TimeZone.kt`
- `core/commonJs/src/UtcOffset.kt`
- `core/jvm/src/TimeZoneJvm.kt`
- `core/jvm/src/UtcOffsetJvm.kt`
- `core/native/src/TimeZone.kt`
- `core/native/src/UtcOffset.kt`

## Instructions

Please analyze this issue and implement a fix. Make the necessary code changes to resolve the problem described above.

Focus on:
- Understanding the root cause of the issue
- Making minimal, targeted changes to fix the problem
- Ensuring your changes don't break existing functionality

## Important Evaluation Notes

**Your work will be evaluated by running hidden tests that you do not have access to.** These tests will verify that your fix correctly addresses the issue. You should be confident in your solution before finishing.
**If you write tests to verify your changes, create them in NEW test files only** - do NOT modify existing test files

## CRITICAL RESTRICTIONS

**NEVER use git commands.** Do not run `git status`, `git diff`, `git checkout`, `git reset`, or ANY other git commands. Using git will corrupt the evaluation environment and invalidate your entire submission. All file operations should be done through normal file editing and reading tools, not git.

1→/*
  2→ * Copyright 2019-2020 JetBrains s.r.o.
  3→ * Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 License that can be found in the LICENSE.txt file.
  4→ */
  5→
  6→package kotlinx.datetime
  7→
  8→import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
  9→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
 10→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.InstantIso8601Serializer
 11→import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 12→import kotlin.time.*
 13→
 14→/**
 15→ * A moment in time.
 16→ *
 17→ * A point in time must be uniquely identified, so that it is independent of a time zone.
 18→ * For example, `1970-01-01, 00:00:00` does not represent a moment in time, since this would happen at different times
 19→ * in different time zones: someone in Tokyo would think its already `1970-01-01` several hours earlier than someone in
 20→ * Berlin would. To represent such entities, use [LocalDateTime].
 21→ * In contrast, "the moment the clocks in London first showed 00:00 on Jan 1, 2000" is a specific moment
 22→ * in time, as is "1970-01-01, 00:00:00 UTC+0", and so it can be represented as an [Instant].
 23→ *
 24→ * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
 25→ * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 26→ * of the day when a leap second happens.
 27→ *
 28→ * Some ways in which [Instant] can be acquired are:
 29→ * - [Clock.now] can be used to query the current moment for the given clock. With [Clock.System], it is the current
 30→ *   moment as the platform sees it.
 31→ * - [Instant.parse] parses an ISO-8601 string.
 32→ * - [Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds] and [Instant.fromEpochSeconds] construct the instant values from the amount of time
 33→ *   since `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` (the Unix epoch).
 34→ */
 35→@Serializable(with = InstantIso8601Serializer::class)
 36→public expect class Instant : Comparable<Instant> {
 37→
 38→    /**
 39→     * The number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` rounded down to a [Long] number.
 40→     *
 41→     * The difference between the rounded number of seconds and the actual number of seconds
 42→     * is returned by [nanosecondsOfSecond] property expressed in nanoseconds.
 43→     *
 44→     * Note that this number doesn't include leap seconds added or removed since the epoch.
 45→     *
 46→     * @see Instant.fromEpochSeconds
 47→     */
 48→    public val epochSeconds: Long
 49→
 50→    /**
 51→     * The number of nanoseconds by which this instant is later than [epochSeconds] from the epoch instant.
 52→     *
 53→     * The value is always positive and lies in the range `0..999_999_999`.
 54→     *
 55→     * @see Instant.fromEpochSeconds
 56→     */
 57→    public val nanosecondsOfSecond: Int
 58→
 59→    /**
 60→     * Returns the number of milliseconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`.
 61→     *
 62→     * Any fractional part of millisecond is rounded down to the whole number of milliseconds.
 63→     *
 64→     * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
 65→     *
 66→     * @see Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds
 67→     */
 68→    public fun toEpochMilliseconds(): Long
 69→
 70→    /**
 71→     * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the specified [duration] to this instant.
 72→     *
 73→     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
 74→     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
 75→     *
 76→     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
 77→     */
 78→    public operator fun plus(duration: Duration): Instant
 79→
 80→    /**
 81→     * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the specified [duration] from this instant.
 82→     *
 83→     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
 84→     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
 85→     *
 86→     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
 87→     */
 88→    public operator fun minus(duration: Duration): Instant
 89→
 90→    // questionable
 91→    /**
 92→     * Returns the [Duration] between two instants: [other] and `this`.
 93→     *
 94→     * The duration returned is positive if this instant is later than the other,
 95→     * and negative if this instant is earlier than the other.
 96→     *
 97→     * The result is never clamped, but note that for instants that are far apart,
 98→     * the value returned may represent the duration between them inexactly due to the loss of precision.
 99→     */
100→    public operator fun minus(other: Instant): Duration
101→
102→    /**
103→     * Compares `this` instant with the [other] instant.
104→     * Returns zero if this instant represents the same moment as the other (i.e. equal to other),
105→     * a negative number if this instant is earlier than the other,
106→     * and a positive number if this instant is later than the other.
107→     */
108→    public override operator fun compareTo(other: Instant): Int
109→
110→    /**
111→     * Converts this instant to the ISO-8601 string representation.
112→     *
113→     * The representation uses the UTC-SLS time scale, instead of UTC.
114→     * In practice, this means that leap second handling will not be readjusted to the UTC.
115→     * Leap seconds will not be added or skipped, so it is impossible to acquire a string
116→     * where the component for seconds is 60, and for any day, it's possible to observe 23:59:59.
117→     *
118→     * @see Instant.parse
119→     * @see DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET for a very similar format. The difference is that
120→     * [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] will not add trailing zeros for readability to the
121→     * fractional part of the second.
122→     */
123→    public override fun toString(): String
124→
125→
126→    public companion object {
127→        @Deprecated("Use Clock.System.now() instead", ReplaceWith("Clock.System.now()", "kotlinx.datetime.Clock"), level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR)
128→        public fun now(): Instant
129→
130→        /**
131→         * Returns an [Instant] that is [epochMilliseconds] number of milliseconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`.
132→         *
133→         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
134→         *
135→         * @see Instant.toEpochMilliseconds
136→         */
137→        public fun fromEpochMilliseconds(epochMilliseconds: Long): Instant
138→
139→        /**
140→         * Returns an [Instant] that is the [epochSeconds] number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`
141→         * and the [nanosecondAdjustment] number of nanoseconds from the whole second.
142→         *
143→         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
144→         */
145→        public fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Long = 0): Instant
146→
147→        /**
148→         * Returns an [Instant] that is the [epochSeconds] number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`
149→         * and the [nanosecondAdjustment] number of nanoseconds from the whole second.
150→         *
151→         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
152→         */
153→        public fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Int): Instant
154→
155→        /**
156→         * A shortcut for calling [DateTimeFormat.parse], followed by [DateTimeComponents.toInstantUsingOffset].
157→         *
158→         * Parses a string that represents an instant including date and time components and a mandatory
159→         * time zone offset and returns the parsed [Instant] value.
160→         *
161→         * The string is considered to represent time on the UTC-SLS time scale instead of UTC.
162→         * In practice, this means that, even if there is a leap second on the given day, it will not affect how the
163→         * time is parsed, even if it's in the last 1000 seconds of the day.
164→         * Instead, even if there is a negative leap second on the given day, 23:59:59 is still considered valid time.
165→         * 23:59:60 is invalid on UTC-SLS, so parsing it will fail.
166→         *
167→         * If the format is not specified, [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] is used.
168→         *
169→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [Instant] are exceeded.
170→         *
171→         * @see Instant.toString for formatting using the default format.
172→         * @see Instant.format for formatting using a custom format.
173→         */
174→        public fun parse(
175→            input: CharSequence,
176→            format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents> = DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET
177→        ): Instant
178→
179→        /**
180→         * An instant value that is far in the past.
181→         *
182→         * All instants in the range `DISTANT_PAST..DISTANT_FUTURE` can be [converted][Instant.toLocalDateTime] to
183→         * [LocalDateTime] without exceptions on all supported platforms.
184→         */
185→        public val DISTANT_PAST: Instant // -100001-12-31T23:59:59.999999999Z
186→
187→        /**
188→         * An instant value that is far in the future.
189→         *
190→         * All instants in the range `DISTANT_PAST..DISTANT_FUTURE` can be [converted][Instant.toLocalDateTime] to
191→         * [LocalDateTime] without exceptions on all supported platforms.
192→         */
193→        public val DISTANT_FUTURE: Instant // +100000-01-01T00:00:00Z
194→
195→        internal val MIN: Instant
196→        internal val MAX: Instant
197→    }
198→}
199→
200→/** Returns true if the instant is [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] or earlier. */
201→public val Instant.isDistantPast: Boolean
202→    get() = this <= Instant.DISTANT_PAST
203→
204→/** Returns true if the instant is [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE] or later. */
205→public val Instant.isDistantFuture: Boolean
206→    get() = this >= Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE
207→
208→/**
209→ * @suppress
210→ */
211→@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("Instant.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
212→public fun String.toInstant(): Instant = Instant.parse(this)
213→
214→/**
215→ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding components of [DateTimePeriod] to this instant. The components are
216→ * added in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e. from years to nanoseconds.
217→ *
218→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the results of intermediate computations are too large to fit in
219→ * [LocalDateTime].
220→ */
221→public expect fun Instant.plus(period: DateTimePeriod, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
222→
223→/**
224→ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting components of [DateTimePeriod] from this instant. The components
225→ * are subtracted in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e. from years to nanoseconds.
226→ *
227→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the results of intermediate computations are too large to fit in
228→ * [LocalDateTime].
229→ */
230→public fun Instant.minus(period: DateTimePeriod, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
231→    /* An overflow can happen for any component, but we are only worried about nanoseconds, as having an overflow in
232→    any other component means that `plus` will throw due to the minimum value of the numeric type overflowing the
233→    platform-specific limits. */
234→    if (period.totalNanoseconds != Long.MIN_VALUE) {
235→        val negatedPeriod = with(period) { buildDateTimePeriod(-totalMonths, -days, -totalNanoseconds) }
236→        plus(negatedPeriod, timeZone)
237→    } else {

 47→         * - a string starting with '+', '-', `UTC+`, `UTC-`, `UT+`, `UT-`, `GMT+`, `GMT-` — identifiers the time zone
 48→         *   with the fixed offset specified after `+` or `-`,

 50→         * In the IANA Time Zone Database (TZDB) which is used as the default source of time zones,

 6→package kotlinx.datetime
 7→
 8→import kotlin.time.*
 9→
10→/**
11→ * A source of [Instant] values.
12→ *
13→ * See [Clock.System][Clock.System] for the clock instance that queries the operating system.
14→ */
15→public interface Clock {
16→    /**
17→     * Returns the [Instant] corresponding to the current time, according to this clock.
18→     */
19→    public fun now(): Instant
20→
21→    /**
22→     * The [Clock] instance that queries the operating system as its source of knowledge of time.
23→     */
24→    public object System : Clock {
25→        override fun now(): Instant = @Suppress("DEPRECATION_ERROR") Instant.now()
26→    }
27→
28→    public companion object {
29→
30→    }
31→}
32→
33→/**
34→ * Returns the current date at the given [time zone][timeZone], according to [this Clock][this].
35→ */
36→public fun Clock.todayIn(timeZone: TimeZone): LocalDate =
37→    now().toLocalDateTime(timeZone).date
38→
39→/**
40→ * Returns a [TimeSource] that uses this [Clock] to mark a time instant and to find the amount of time elapsed since that mark.
41→ */
42→@ExperimentalTime
43→public fun Clock.asTimeSource(): TimeSource.WithComparableMarks = object : TimeSource.WithComparableMarks {
44→    override fun markNow(): ComparableTimeMark = InstantTimeMark(now(), this@asTimeSource)
45→}
46→
47→@ExperimentalTime
48→private class InstantTimeMark(private val instant: Instant, private val clock: Clock) : ComparableTimeMark {
49→    override fun elapsedNow(): Duration = saturatingDiff(clock.now(), instant)
50→
51→    override fun plus(duration: Duration): ComparableTimeMark = InstantTimeMark(instant.saturatingAdd(duration), clock)
52→    override fun minus(duration: Duration): ComparableTimeMark = InstantTimeMark(instant.saturatingAdd(-duration), clock)
53→
54→    override fun minus(other: ComparableTimeMark): Duration {
55→        if (other !is InstantTimeMark || other.clock != this.clock) {
56→            throw IllegalArgumentException("Subtracting or comparing time marks from different time sources is not possible: $this and $other")
57→        }
58→        return saturatingDiff(this.instant, other.instant)
59→    }
60→
61→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
62→        return other is InstantTimeMark && this.clock == other.clock && this.instant == other.instant
63→    }
64→
65→    override fun hashCode(): Int = instant.hashCode()
66→
67→    override fun toString(): String = "InstantTimeMark($instant, $clock)"
68→
69→    private fun Instant.isSaturated() = this == Instant.MAX || this == Instant.MIN
70→    private fun Instant.saturatingAdd(duration: Duration): Instant {
71→        if (isSaturated()) {
72→            if (duration.isInfinite() && duration.isPositive() != this.isDistantFuture) {
73→                throw IllegalArgumentException("Summing infinities of different signs")
74→            }
75→            return this
76→        }
77→        return this + duration
78→    }
79→    private fun saturatingDiff(instant1: Instant, instant2: Instant): Duration = when {
80→        instant1 == instant2 ->
81→            Duration.ZERO
82→        instant1.isSaturated() || instant2.isSaturated() ->
83→            (instant1 - instant2) * Double.POSITIVE_INFINITY
84→        else ->
85→            instant1 - instant2
86→    }
87→}
88→
89→@Deprecated("Use Clock.todayIn instead", ReplaceWith("this.todayIn(timeZone)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
90→public fun Clock.todayAt(timeZone: TimeZone): LocalDate = todayIn(timeZone)

 50→         * LocalDateTime.Format { date(LocalDate.Formats.ISO); char(' '); time(LocalTime.Formats.ISO) }
 51→         *
 52→         * // `08/30 18:43:13`, using a custom format:
 53→         * LocalDateTime.Format {
 54→         *   monthNumber(); char('/'); dayOfMonth()
 55→         *   char(' ')
 56→         *   hour(); char(':'); minute()
 57→         *   optional { char(':'); second() }

 64→         * There is a collection of predefined formats in [LocalDateTime.Formats].

 66→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if parsing using this format is ambiguous.

 81→     * [kotlinx.datetime.format.DateTimeFormat] for [LocalDateTime] values.

163→    /** Returns the number-of-month (1..12) component of the date. */

174→
175→    /** Returns the day-of-year component of the date. */

177→

180→

 12→/**
 13→ * The date part of [LocalDateTime].
 14→ *
 15→ * This class represents dates without a reference to a particular time zone.
 16→ * As such, these objects may denote different spans of time in different time zones: for someone in Berlin,
 17→ * `2020-08-30` started and ended at different moments from those for someone in Tokyo.
 18→ *
 19→ * The arithmetic on [LocalDate] values is defined independently of the time zone (so `2020-08-30` plus one day
 20→ * is `2020-08-31` everywhere): see various [LocalDate.plus] and [LocalDate.minus] functions, as well
 21→ * as [LocalDate.periodUntil] and various other [*until][LocalDate.daysUntil] functions.
 22→ */
 23→@Serializable(with = LocalDateIso8601Serializer::class)
 24→public expect class LocalDate : Comparable<LocalDate> {
 25→    public companion object {
 26→        /**
 27→         * A shortcut for calling [DateTimeFormat.parse].
 28→         *
 29→         * Parses a string that represents a date and returns the parsed [LocalDate] value.
 30→         *
 31→         * If [format] is not specified, [Formats.ISO] is used.
 32→         *
 33→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [LocalDate] are exceeded.
 34→         *
 35→         * @see LocalDate.toString for formatting using the default format.
 36→         * @see LocalDate.format for formatting using a custom format.
 37→         */
 38→        public fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<LocalDate> = getIsoDateFormat()): LocalDate
 39→
 40→        /**
 41→         * Returns a [LocalDate] that is [epochDays] number of days from the epoch day `1970-01-01`.
 42→         *
 43→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the result exceeds the platform-specific boundaries of [LocalDate].
 44→         *
 45→         * @see LocalDate.toEpochDays
 46→         */
 47→        public fun fromEpochDays(epochDays: Int): LocalDate
 48→
 49→        /**
 50→         * Creates a new format for parsing and formatting [LocalDate] values.
 51→         *
 52→         * Example:
 53→         * ```
 54→         * // 2020 Jan 05
 55→         * LocalDate.Format {
 56→         *   year()
 57→         *   char(' ')
 58→         *   monthName(MonthNames.ENGLISH_ABBREVIATED)
 59→         *   char(' ')
 60→         *   dayOfMonth()
 61→         * }
 62→         * ```
 63→         *
 64→         * Only parsing and formatting of well-formed values is supported. If the input does not fit the boundaries
 65→         * (for example, [dayOfMonth] is 31 for February), consider using [DateTimeComponents.Format] instead.
 66→         *
 67→         * There is a collection of predefined formats in [LocalDate.Formats].
 68→         *
 69→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if parsing using this format is ambiguous.
 70→         */
 71→        @Suppress("FunctionName")
 72→        public fun Format(block: DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDate.() -> Unit): DateTimeFormat<LocalDate>

 75→        internal val MAX: LocalDate
 76→    }

 78→    /**
 79→     * A collection of predefined formats for parsing and formatting [LocalDate] values.
 80→     *
 81→     * See [LocalDate.Formats.ISO] and [LocalDate.Formats.ISO_BASIC] for popular predefined formats.
 82→     * [LocalDate.parse] and [LocalDate.toString] can be used as convenient shortcuts for the
 83→     * [LocalDate.Formats.ISO] format.

 86→     * [kotlinx.datetime.format.DateTimeFormat] for [LocalDate] values.

 88→    public object Formats {
 89→        /**
 90→         * ISO 8601 extended format, which is the format used by [LocalDate.toString] and [LocalDate.parse].
 91→         *
 92→         * Examples of dates in ISO 8601 format:
 93→         * - `2020-08-30`
 94→         * - `+12020-08-30`
 95→         * - `0000-08-30`
 96→         * - `-0001-08-30`
 97→         */
 98→        public val ISO: DateTimeFormat<LocalDate>

100→        /**
101→         * ISO 8601 basic format.
102→         *
103→         * Examples of dates in ISO 8601 basic format:
104→         * - `20200830`
105→         * - `+120200830`
106→         * - `00000830`
107→         * - `-00010830`
108→         */

110→    }
111→
112→    /**
113→     * Constructs a [LocalDate] instance from the given date components.
114→     *
115→     * The components [monthNumber] and [dayOfMonth] are 1-based.
116→     *
117→     * The supported ranges of components:
118→     * - [year] the range is platform dependent, but at least is enough to represent dates of all instants between
119→     *          [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] and [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE]
120→     * - [monthNumber] `1..12`
121→     * - [dayOfMonth] `1..31`, the upper bound can be less, depending on the month
122→     *
123→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any parameter is out of range, or if [dayOfMonth] is invalid for the

125→     */
126→    public constructor(year: Int, monthNumber: Int, dayOfMonth: Int)

128→    /**
129→     * Constructs a [LocalDate] instance from the given date components.
130→     *
131→     * The supported ranges of components:
132→     * - [year] the range is platform dependent, but at least is enough to represent dates of all instants between
133→     *          [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] and [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE]

135→     * - [dayOfMonth] `1..31`, the upper bound can be less, depending on the month
136→     *

143→    public val year: Int
144→
145→    /** Returns the number-of-month (1..12) component of the date. */
146→    public val monthNumber: Int

148→    /** Returns the month ([Month]) component of the date. */

171→     * Returns zero if this date represent the same day as the other (i.e. equal to other),
172→     * a negative number if this date is earlier than the other,
173→     * and a positive number if this date is later than the other.
174→     */
175→    public override fun compareTo(other: LocalDate): Int
176→
177→    /**
178→     * Converts this date to the extended ISO-8601 string representation.
179→     *
180→     * @see Formats.ISO for the format details.
181→     * @see parse for the dual operation: obtaining [LocalDate] from a string.
182→     * @see LocalDate.format for formatting using a custom format.
183→     */

188→ * Formats this value using the given [format].
189→ * Equivalent to calling [DateTimeFormat.format] on [format] with `this`.
190→ */

193→/**

199→/**
200→ * Combines this date's components with the specified time components into a [LocalDateTime] value.
201→ *
202→ * For finding an instant that corresponds to the start of a date in a particular time zone consider using
203→ * [LocalDate.atStartOfDayIn] function because a day does not always start at the fixed time 0:00:00.
204→ */
205→public fun LocalDate.atTime(hour: Int, minute: Int, second: Int = 0, nanosecond: Int = 0): LocalDateTime =
206→    LocalDateTime(year, monthNumber, dayOfMonth, hour, minute, second, nanosecond)
207→
208→/**
209→ * Combines this date's components with the specified [LocalTime] components into a [LocalDateTime] value.
210→ *
211→ * For finding an instant that corresponds to the start of a date in a particular time zone consider using
212→ * [LocalDate.atStartOfDayIn] function because a day does not always start at the fixed time 0:00:00.
213→ */
214→public fun LocalDate.atTime(time: LocalTime): LocalDateTime = LocalDateTime(this, time)

217→/**
218→ * Returns a date that is the result of adding components of [DatePeriod] to this date. The components are
219→ * added in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e. from years to days.
220→ *
221→ * @see LocalDate.periodUntil
222→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the results of intermediate computations are too large to fit in
223→ * [LocalDate].
224→ */
225→public expect operator fun LocalDate.plus(period: DatePeriod): LocalDate

227→/**
228→ * Returns a date that is the result of subtracting components of [DatePeriod] from this date. The components are
229→ * subtracted in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e. from years to days.
230→ *
231→ * @see LocalDate.periodUntil
232→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the results of intermediate computations are too large to fit in
233→ * [LocalDate].
234→ */
235→public operator fun LocalDate.minus(period: DatePeriod): LocalDate =
236→    if (period.days != Int.MIN_VALUE && period.months != Int.MIN_VALUE) {
237→        plus(with(period) { DatePeriod(-years, -months, -days) })
238→    } else {

240→            .minus(period.days, DateTimeUnit.DAY)
241→    }

243→/**
244→ * Returns a [DatePeriod] representing the difference between `this` and [other] dates.
245→ *
246→ * The components of [DatePeriod] are calculated so that adding it to `this` date results in the [other] date.

253→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if the number of months between the two dates exceeds an Int (JVM only).

261→ *
262→ * The components of [DatePeriod] are calculated so that adding it back to the `other` date results in this date.

 22→ * Therefore, even on the same date, [LocalTime] denotes different moments of time.
 23→ * For example, `18:43` happens at different moments in Berlin and in Tokyo.

 26→ * transitions it may give misleading results.

 64→         * with 86400 being the number of seconds in a calendar day.

{"file_path":"/project/core/common/src/Instant.kt","old_string":"/**
 * A moment in time.
 *
 * A point in time must be uniquely identified, so that it is independent of a time zone.
 * For example, `1970-01-01, 00:00:00` does not represent a moment in time, since this would happen at different times
 * in different time zones: someone in Tokyo would think its already `1970-01-01` several hours earlier than someone in
 * Berlin would. To represent such entities, use [LocalDateTime].
 * In contrast, "the moment the clocks in London first showed 00:00 on Jan 1, 2000" is a specific moment
 * in time, as is "1970-01-01, 00:00:00 UTC+0", and so it can be represented as an [Instant].
 *
 * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
 * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 * of the day when a leap second happens.
 *
 * Some ways in which [Instant] can be acquired are:
 * - [Clock.now] can be used to query the current moment for the given clock. With [Clock.System], it is the current
 *   moment as the platform sees it.
 * - [Instant.parse] parses an ISO-8601 string.
 * - [Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds] and [Instant.fromEpochSeconds] construct the instant values from the amount of time
 *   since `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` (the Unix epoch).
 */","new_string":"/**
 * A moment in time, independent of time zones.
 *
 * A point in time must be uniquely identified regardless of where it is observed. For example,
 * `1970-01-01, 00:00:00` does not represent a moment in time, since it happens at different moments in
 * different time zones: someone in Tokyo would observe it earlier than someone in Berlin. To represent such
 * local clock readings, use [LocalDateTime]. In contrast, "the moment the clocks in London first showed 00:00
 * on Jan 1, 2000" is a specific moment in time, as is "1970-01-01, 00:00:00 UTC+0", and so it can be represented
 * as an [Instant].
 *
 * ## Time scale and ISO-8601
 *
 * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
 * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 * of the day when a leap second happens. Consequently, [Instant.toString] and [Instant.parse] use an ISO-8601
 * representation on the UTC-SLS scale and never include `:60` in the seconds component.
 *
 * ## How to acquire
 *
 * - [Clock.now] can be used to query the current moment for the given clock. With [Clock.System], it is the current
 *   moment as the platform sees it.
 * - [Instant.parse] parses an ISO-8601 string (with an explicit offset).
 * - [Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds] and [Instant.fromEpochSeconds] construct values from the amount of time since
 *   `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` (the Unix epoch).
 *
 * ## Usage and interaction with other types
 *
 * Use [Instant] to perform timeline-based computations and to convert to civil time in a time zone:
 * - Use [TimeZone] conversions to obtain [LocalDateTime] or [LocalDate]: `instant.toLocalDateTime(timeZone)`.
 * - Use [DateTimePeriod] and [DateTimeUnit.DateBased] with a [TimeZone] for calendar-aware arithmetic.
 * - Use [Duration] and [DateTimeUnit.TimeBased] for timeline-based arithmetic (independent of time zones).
 *
 * A common pitfall is to confuse calendar arithmetic with duration arithmetic: adding `1.days` to an [Instant]
 * advances the timeline by 24 hours, which may or may not correspond to a change of date in a particular time zone.
 * For calendar-aware operations, use [Instant.plus] with a [DateTimePeriod] or a date-based [DateTimeUnit] and an
 * explicit [TimeZone].
 *
 * ## Basic example
 * ```kotlin
 * val now = Clock.System.now()
 * val berlin = TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")
 * val local = now.toLocalDateTime(berlin)
 * val tomorrowInBerlin = now.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, berlin)
 * ```
 *
 * ## Platform interop
 *
 * Platform-specific conversions (for example, to and from `java.time.Instant` on the JVM or `NSDate` on Darwin)
 * are provided in the corresponding platform source sets.
 */"}

 * A time zone, provides the conversion between [Instant] and [LocalDateTime] values
 * using a collection of rules specifying which [LocalDateTime] value corresponds to each [Instant].
 */","new_string":"/**
 * A time zone, providing conversions between [Instant] and [LocalDateTime] values
 * using a collection of rules specifying which civil date/time corresponds to each moment on the timeline.
 *
 * Time zones can be **fixed-offset** (always the same [UtcOffset]) or **region-based** (rules that include
 * daylight-saving transitions and historical changes). The default source of time zones is the IANA Time Zone
 * Database (TZDB) on all supported platforms.
 *
 * ## How to acquire
 *
 * - [TimeZone.currentSystemDefault] to use the system-configured time zone at the moment of the call.
 * - [TimeZone.of] to resolve a specific zone identifier (for example, `Europe/Berlin` or `UTC+02:00`).
 * - [TimeZone.UTC] for the fixed UTC time zone.
 * - [UtcOffset.asTimeZone] to build a fixed-offset time zone from an offset.
 *
 * ## Usage and conversions
 *
 * - Convert an [Instant] to a human-readable local date/time with [Instant.toLocalDateTime].
 * - Convert a [LocalDateTime] to a timeline [Instant] with [LocalDateTime.toInstant].
 * - Resolve offsets for a moment using [TimeZone.offsetAt] or [Instant.offsetIn].
 *
 * These conversions may be **ambiguous** or **non-existent** around time-zone transitions (for example, when
 * clocks are moved forward or backward). See [LocalDateTime.toInstant] for the resolution rules.
 *
 * ## Basic example
 * ```kotlin
 * val timeZone = TimeZone.of("America/Los_Angeles")
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val local = instant.toLocalDateTime(timeZone)
 * val offset = timeZone.offsetAt(instant)
 * ```
 */"}

 */","new_string":"/**
 * The representation of a specific civil date and time without a reference to a particular time zone.
 *
 * This class does not describe specific *moments in time*, which are represented as [Instant] values. Instead, its
 * instances can be thought of as clock readings, something that an observer in a particular time zone could witness.
 * For example, `2020-08-30T18:43` is not a *moment in time*, since someone in Berlin and someone in Tokyo would witness
 * this on their clocks at different moments.
 *
 * The main purpose of this class is to provide human-readable representations of [Instant] values, or to transfer them
 * as data between systems. It follows the ISO-8601 calendar system for date/time components and does not contain any
 * offset or time-zone information.
 *
 * ## How to acquire
 *
 * - Use [Instant.toLocalDateTime] with a [TimeZone] to obtain a local date/time for a specific moment.
 * - Parse from a string with [LocalDateTime.parse].
 * - Construct manually from components or from [LocalDate] and [LocalTime].
 *
 * ## Usage and interaction with other types
 *
 * - Convert to an [Instant] with [LocalDateTime.toInstant], providing a [TimeZone] or [UtcOffset].
 * - Extract [date] and [time] parts for calendar logic.
 * - Use [LocalDate] arithmetic when you only need date-based calculations.
 *
 * The arithmetic on [LocalDateTime] values is not provided, since without accounting for time-zone transitions it may
 * give misleading results. If you need to move across calendar units in a time zone, convert to an [Instant] and use
 * [Instant.plus] with a [TimeZone].
 *
 * ## Basic example
 * ```kotlin
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val local = instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.UTC)
 * val roundTrip = local.toInstant(TimeZone.UTC)
 * ```
 */"}

{"file_path":"/project/core/common/src/LocalDate.kt","old_string":"/**
 * The date part of [LocalDateTime].
 *
 * This class represents dates without a reference to a particular time zone.
 * As such, these objects may denote different spans of time in different time zones: for someone in Berlin,
 * `2020-08-30` started and ended at different moments from those for someone in Tokyo.
 *
 * The arithmetic on [LocalDate] values is defined independently of the time zone (so `2020-08-30` plus one day
 * is `2020-08-31` everywhere): see various [LocalDate.plus] and [LocalDate.minus] functions, as well
 * as [LocalDate.periodUntil] and various other [*until][LocalDate.daysUntil] functions.
 */","new_string":"/**
 * The date part of [LocalDateTime].
 *
 * This class represents dates without a reference to a particular time zone. As such, these objects may denote
 * different spans of time in different time zones: for someone in Berlin, `2020-08-30` started and ended at
 * different moments from those for someone in Tokyo.
 *
 * The calendar system used is ISO-8601 (proleptic Gregorian calendar). [LocalDate] is best used for expressing
 * calendar concepts such as birthdays, deadlines, or daily schedules, independent of a specific time zone.
 *
 * ## How to acquire
 *
 * - Use [LocalDate.parse] to parse an ISO-8601 string.
 * - Use [Instant.toLocalDateTime] with a [TimeZone] and then take [LocalDateTime.date].
 * - Use [Clock.todayIn] to obtain the current date in a time zone.
 *
 * ## Usage
 *
 * - Use [LocalDate.plus], [LocalDate.minus], and [LocalDate.periodUntil] for calendar arithmetic.
 * - Combine with a [LocalTime] using [LocalDate.atTime] to create a [LocalDateTime].
 * - Convert to a timeline moment with [LocalDate.atStartOfDayIn] if you need the start of day in a time zone.
 *
 * The arithmetic on [LocalDate] values is defined independently of the time zone (so `2020-08-30` plus one day
 * is `2020-08-31` everywhere): see various [LocalDate.plus] and [LocalDate.minus] functions, as well as
 * [LocalDate.periodUntil] and various other [*until][LocalDate.daysUntil] functions.
 *
 * ## Basic example
 * ```kotlin
 * val date = LocalDate.parse("2020-08-30")
 * val nextWeek = date.plus(7, DateTimeUnit.DAY)
 * val startOfDay = date.atStartOfDayIn(TimeZone.UTC)
 * ```
 */"}

 27→@Serializable(with = DateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
 28→public sealed class DateTimeUnit {

 30→    /** Produces a date-time unit that is a multiple of this unit times the specified integer [scalar] value. */
 31→    public abstract operator fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit

 33→    /**
 34→     * A date-time unit that has the precise time duration.
 35→     *

 37→     * Any such unit can be represented as some fixed number of nanoseconds.

 43→         */

 49→        init {
 50→            require(nanoseconds > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $nanoseconds ns." }
 51→            // find a concise string representation for the unit with this duration
 52→            when {
 53→                nanoseconds % 3600_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 54→                    unitName = "HOUR"
 55→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 3600_000_000_000
 56→                }
 57→                nanoseconds % 60_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 58→                    unitName = "MINUTE"
 59→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 60_000_000_000
 60→                }
 61→                nanoseconds % 1_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 62→                    unitName = "SECOND"
 63→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000_000_000
 64→                }
 65→                nanoseconds % 1_000_000 == 0L -> {
 66→                    unitName = "MILLISECOND"
 67→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000_000
 68→                }
 69→                nanoseconds % 1_000 == 0L -> {
 70→                    unitName = "MICROSECOND"
 71→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000

 73→                else -> {

 75→                    unitScale = nanoseconds

100→     * require a [TimeZone] to find the corresponding [LocalDateTimes][LocalDateTime] first to perform

117→     *
118→     * The reason lies in time zone transitions, because of which some days can be 23 or 25 hours.
119→     * For example, we say that exactly a whole day has passed between `2019-10-27T02:59` and `2019-10-28T02:59`
120→     * in Berlin, despite the fact that the clocks were turned back one hour, so there are, in fact, 25 hours
121→     * between the two date-times.
122→     */
123→    @Serializable(with = DayBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
124→    public class DayBased(
125→        /**
126→         * The length of this unit in days.
127→         */
128→        public val days: Int
129→    ) : DateBased() {
130→        init {
131→            require(days > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $days days." }
132→        }
133→
134→        override fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit.DayBased = DateTimeUnit.DayBased(safeMultiply(days, scalar))
135→
136→        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
137→            this === other || (other is DateTimeUnit.DayBased && this.days == other.days)
138→
139→        override fun hashCode(): Int = days xor 0x10000
140→
141→        override fun toString(): String = if (days % 7 == 0)
142→            formatToString(days / 7, "WEEK")
143→        else
144→            formatToString(days, "DAY")
145→    }
146→
147→    /**
148→     * A date-time unit equal to some number of months.
149→     *
150→     * Since different months have different number of days, a `MonthBased`-unit cannot be expressed a multiple of some [DayBased]-unit.
151→     */
152→    @Serializable(with = MonthBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
153→    public class MonthBased(
154→        /**
155→         * The length of this unit in months.
156→         */
157→        public val months: Int
158→    ) : DateBased() {
159→        init {
160→            require(months > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $months months." }
161→        }
162→
163→        override fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit.MonthBased = DateTimeUnit.MonthBased(safeMultiply(months, scalar))
164→
165→        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
166→            this === other || (other is DateTimeUnit.MonthBased && this.months == other.months)
167→
168→        override fun hashCode(): Int = months xor 0x20000
169→
170→        override fun toString(): String = when {
171→            months % 12_00 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 12_00, "CENTURY")
172→            months % 12 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 12, "YEAR")
173→            months % 3 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 3, "QUARTER")
174→            else -> formatToString(months, "MONTH")
175→        }
176→    }
177→
178→    protected fun formatToString(value: Int, unit: String): String = if (value == 1) unit else "$value-$unit"
179→    protected fun formatToString(value: Long, unit: String): String = if (value == 1L) unit else "$value-$unit"
180→
181→    public companion object {
182→        /**
183→         * A nanosecond, which is `1/1_000_000_000` of a second.
184→         */
185→        public val NANOSECOND: TimeBased = TimeBased(nanoseconds = 1)
186→
187→        /**
188→         * A microsecond, which is `1/1_000_000` of a second, or `1_000` nanoseconds.
189→         */
190→        public val MICROSECOND: TimeBased = NANOSECOND * 1000
191→
192→        /**
193→         * A millisecond, which is `1/1_000` of a second, or `1_000_000` nanoseconds.
194→         */
195→        public val MILLISECOND: TimeBased = MICROSECOND * 1000
196→
197→        /**
198→         * A second.
199→         */
200→        public val SECOND: TimeBased = MILLISECOND * 1000
201→
202→        /**
203→         * A minute, which is 60 seconds.
204→         */
205→        public val MINUTE: TimeBased = SECOND * 60
206→
207→        /**
208→         * An hour, which is 60 minutes, or 3600 seconds.
209→         */
210→        public val HOUR: TimeBased = MINUTE * 60
211→
212→        /**
213→         * A calendar day.
214→         *
215→         * Note that a calendar day is not the same as 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
216→         */
217→        public val DAY: DayBased = DayBased(days = 1)
218→
219→        /**
220→         * A week, which is 7 [calendar days][DAY].
221→         */
222→        public val WEEK: DayBased = DAY * 7
223→
224→        /**
225→         * A month.
226→         *
227→         * Note that a month doesn't have a constant number of calendar days in it.
228→         */
229→        public val MONTH: MonthBased = MonthBased(months = 1)
230→
231→        /**
232→         * A quarter, which is three [months][MONTH].
233→         */
234→        public val QUARTER: MonthBased = MONTH * 3
235→
236→        /**
237→         * A year, which is 12 [months][MONTH].
238→         */
239→        public val YEAR: MonthBased = MONTH * 12
240→
241→        /**
242→         * A century, which is 100 [years][YEAR], or 1200 [months][MONTH].
243→         */
244→        public val CENTURY: MonthBased = YEAR * 100
245→    }
246→}

1→/*

  3→ * Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 License that can be found in the LICENSE.txt file.
  4→ */
  5→
  6→package kotlinx.datetime
  7→
  8→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
  9→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DatePeriodIso8601Serializer
 10→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer
 11→import kotlin.math.*
 12→import kotlin.time.Duration
 13→import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 14→
 15→/**
 16→ * A difference between two [instants][Instant], decomposed into date and time components.
 17→ *
 18→ * The date components are: [years], [months], [days].
 19→ *
 20→ * The time components are: [hours], [minutes], [seconds], [nanoseconds].
 21→ *
 22→ * A `DateTimePeriod` can be constructed using the same-named constructor function,
 23→ * [parsed][DateTimePeriod.parse] from a string, or returned as the result of instant arithmetic operations (see [Instant.periodUntil]).
 24→ * All these functions can return a [DatePeriod] value, which is a subtype of `DateTimePeriod`,
 25→ * a special case that only stores date components, if all time components of the result happen to be zero.
 26→ */
 27→@Serializable(with = DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
 28→// TODO: could be error-prone without explicitly named params
 29→public sealed class DateTimePeriod {
 30→    internal abstract val totalMonths: Int
 31→
 32→    /**
 33→     * The number of calendar days.
 34→     *
 35→     * Note that a calendar day is not identical to 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
 36→     */
 37→    public abstract val days: Int
 38→    internal abstract val totalNanoseconds: Long
 39→
 40→    /**
 41→     * The number of whole years.
 42→     */
 43→    public val years: Int get() = totalMonths / 12
 44→
 45→    /**
 46→     * The number of months in this period that don't form a whole year, so this value is always in `(-11..11)`.
 47→     */
 48→    public val months: Int get() = totalMonths % 12
 49→
 50→    /**
 51→     * The number of whole hours in this period.
 52→     */
 53→    public open val hours: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds / 3_600_000_000_000).toInt()
 54→
 55→    /**
 56→     * The number of whole minutes in this period that don't form a whole hour, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
 57→     */
 58→    public open val minutes: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 3_600_000_000_000) / 60_000_000_000).toInt()
 59→
 60→    /**
 61→     * The number of whole seconds in this period that don't form a whole minute, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
 62→     */
 63→    public open val seconds: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 60_000_000_000) / NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
 64→
 65→    /**
 66→     * The number of whole nanoseconds in this period that don't form a whole second, so this value is always in
 67→     * `(-999_999_999..999_999_999)`.
 68→     */
 69→    public open val nanoseconds: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
 70→
 71→    private fun allNonpositive() =
 72→        totalMonths <= 0 && days <= 0 && totalNanoseconds <= 0 && (totalMonths or days != 0 || totalNanoseconds != 0L)
 73→
 74→    /**
 75→     * Converts this period to the ISO-8601 string representation for durations.
 76→     *
 77→     * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
 78→     */
 79→    override fun toString(): String = buildString {
 80→        val sign = if (allNonpositive()) { append('-'); -1 } else 1
 81→        append('P')
 82→        if (years != 0) append(years * sign).append('Y')
 83→        if (months != 0) append(months * sign).append('M')
 84→        if (days != 0) append(days * sign).append('D')
 85→        var t = "T"
 86→        if (hours != 0) append(t).append(hours * sign).append('H').also { t = "" }
 87→        if (minutes != 0) append(t).append(minutes * sign).append('M').also { t = "" }
 88→        if (seconds or nanoseconds != 0) {
 89→            append(t)
 90→            append(when {
 91→                seconds != 0 -> seconds * sign
 92→                nanoseconds * sign < 0 -> "-0"
 93→                else -> "0"
 94→            })
 95→            if (nanoseconds != 0) append('.').append((nanoseconds.absoluteValue).toString().padStart(9, '0'))
 96→            append('S')
 97→        }
 98→
 99→        if (length == 1) append("0D")
100→    }
101→
102→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
103→        if (this === other) return true
104→        if (other !is DateTimePeriod) return false
105→
106→        if (totalMonths != other.totalMonths) return false
107→        if (days != other.days) return false
108→        if (totalNanoseconds != other.totalNanoseconds) return false
109→
110→        return true
111→    }
112→
113→    override fun hashCode(): Int {
114→        var result = totalMonths
115→        result = 31 * result + days
116→        result = 31 * result + totalNanoseconds.hashCode()
117→        return result
118→    }
119→
120→    public companion object {
121→        /**
122→         * Parses a ISO-8601 duration string as a [DateTimePeriod].
123→         * If the time components are absent or equal to zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
124→         *
125→         * Additionally, we support the `W` signifier to represent weeks.
126→         *
127→         * Examples of durations in the ISO-8601 format:
128→         * - `P1Y40D` is one year and 40 days
129→         * - `-P1DT1H` is minus (one day and one hour)
130→         * - `P1DT-1H` is one day minus one hour
131→         * - `-PT0.000000001S` is minus one nanosecond
132→         *
133→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [DateTimePeriod] are
134→         * exceeded.
135→         */
136→        public fun parse(text: String): DateTimePeriod {
137→            fun parseException(message: String, position: Int): Nothing =
138→                throw DateTimeFormatException("Parse error at char $position: $message")
139→            val START = 0
140→            val AFTER_P = 1
141→            val AFTER_YEAR = 2
142→            val AFTER_MONTH = 3
143→            val AFTER_WEEK = 4
144→            val AFTER_DAY = 5
145→            val AFTER_T = 6
146→            val AFTER_HOUR = 7
147→            val AFTER_MINUTE = 8
148→            val AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO = 9
149→
150→            var state = START
151→            // next unread character
152→            var i = 0
153→            var sign = 1
154→            var years = 0
155→            var months = 0
156→            var weeks = 0
157→            var days = 0
158→            var hours = 0
159→            var minutes = 0
160→            var seconds = 0
161→            var nanoseconds = 0
162→            while (true) {
163→                if (i >= text.length) {
164→                    if (state == START)
165→                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; 'P' designator is required", i)
166→                    if (state == AFTER_T)
167→                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; at least one time component is required after 'T'", i)
168→                    val daysTotal = when (val n = days.toLong() + weeks * 7) {
169→                        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> n.toInt()
170→                        else -> parseException("The total number of days under 'D' and 'W' designators should fit into an Int", 0)
171→                    }
172→                    return DateTimePeriod(years, months, daysTotal, hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds.toLong())
173→                }
174→                if (state == START) {
175→                    if (i + 1 >= text.length && (text[i] == '+' || text[i] == '-'))
176→                        parseException("Unexpected end of string; 'P' designator is required", i)
177→                    when (text[i]) {
178→                        '+', '-' -> {
179→                            if (text[i] == '-')
180→                                sign = -1
181→                            if (text[i + 1] != 'P')
182→                                parseException("Expected 'P', got '${text[i + 1]}'", i + 1)
183→                            i += 2
184→                        }
185→                        'P' -> { i += 1 }
186→                        else -> parseException("Expected '+', '-', 'P', got '${text[i]}'", i)
187→                    }
188→                    state = AFTER_P
189→                    continue
190→                }
191→                var localSign = sign
192→                val iStart = i
193→                when (text[i]) {
194→                    '+', '-' -> {
195→                        if (text[i] == '-') localSign *= -1
196→                        i += 1
197→                        if (i >= text.length || text[i] !in '0'..'9')
198→                            parseException("A number expected after '${text[i]}'", i)
199→                    }
200→                    in '0'..'9' -> { }
201→                    'T' -> {
202→                        if (state >= AFTER_T)
203→                            parseException("Only one 'T' designator is allowed", i)
204→                        state = AFTER_T
205→                        i += 1
206→                        continue
207→                    }
208→                }
209→                var number = 0L
210→                while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9') {
211→                    try {
212→                        number = safeAdd(safeMultiply(number, 10), (text[i] - '0').toLong())
213→                    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
214→                        parseException("The number is too large", iStart)
215→                    }
216→                    i += 1
217→                }
218→                number *= localSign
219→                if (i == text.length)
220→                    parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
221→                val wrongOrder = "Wrong component order: should be 'Y', 'M', 'W', 'D', then designator 'T', then 'H', 'M', 'S'"
222→                fun Long.toIntThrowing(component: Char): Int {
223→                    if (this < Int.MIN_VALUE || this > Int.MAX_VALUE)
224→                        parseException("Value $this does not fit into an Int, which is required for component '$component'", iStart)
225→                    return toInt()
226→                }
227→                when (text[i].uppercaseChar()) {
228→                    'Y' -> {
229→                        if (state >= AFTER_YEAR)
230→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
231→                        state = AFTER_YEAR
232→                        years = number.toIntThrowing('Y')
233→                    }
234→                    'M' -> {
235→                        if (state >= AFTER_T) {
236→                            // Minutes
237→                            if (state >= AFTER_MINUTE)
238→                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
239→                            state = AFTER_MINUTE
240→                            minutes = number.toIntThrowing('M')
241→                        } else {
242→                            // Months
243→                            if (state >= AFTER_MONTH)
244→                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
245→                            state = AFTER_MONTH
246→                            months = number.toIntThrowing('M')
247→                        }
248→                    }
249→                    'W' -> {
250→                        if (state >= AFTER_WEEK)
251→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
252→                        state = AFTER_WEEK
253→                        weeks = number.toIntThrowing('W')
254→                    }
255→                    'D' -> {
256→                        if (state >= AFTER_DAY)
257→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
258→                        state = AFTER_DAY
259→                        days = number.toIntThrowing('D')
260→                    }
261→                    'H' -> {
262→                        if (state >= AFTER_HOUR || state < AFTER_T)
263→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
264→                        state = AFTER_HOUR
265→                        hours = number.toIntThrowing('H')
266→                    }
267→                    'S' -> {
268→                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
269→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
270→                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
271→                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
272→                    }
273→                    '.', ',' -> {
274→                        i += 1
275→                        if (i >= text.length)
276→                            parseException("Expected designator 'S' after ${text[i - 1]}", i)
277→                        val iStartFraction = i
278→                        while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9')
279→                            i += 1
280→                        val fractionLength = i - iStartFraction
281→                        if (fractionLength > 9)
282→                            parseException("Only the nanosecond fractions of a second are supported", iStartFraction)
283→                        val fractionalPart = text.substring(iStartFraction, i) + "0".repeat(9 - fractionLength)
284→                        nanoseconds = fractionalPart.toInt(10) * localSign
285→                        if (text[i] != 'S')
286→                            parseException("Expected the 'S' designator after a fraction", i)
287→                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
288→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
289→                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
290→                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
291→                    }
292→                    else -> parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
293→                }
294→                i += 1
295→           }
296→        }
297→    }
298→}
299→
300→/**
301→ * @suppress
302→ */
303→@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DateTimePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
304→public fun String.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = DateTimePeriod.parse(this)
305→
306→/**
307→ * A special case of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores date components and has all time components equal to zero.
308→ *
309→ * A `DatePeriod` is automatically returned from all constructor functions for [DateTimePeriod] if it turns out that
310→ * the time components are zero.
311→ *
312→ * `DatePeriod` values are used in operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate] and are returned from operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate],
313→ * but they also can be passed anywhere a [DateTimePeriod] is expected.
314→ */
315→@Serializable(with = DatePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
316→public class DatePeriod internal constructor(
317→    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
318→    override val days: Int,
319→) : DateTimePeriod() {
320→    public constructor(years: Int = 0, months: Int = 0, days: Int = 0): this(totalMonths(years, months), days)
321→    // avoiding excessive computations
322→    /** The number of whole hours in this period. Always equal to zero. */
323→    override val hours: Int get() = 0
324→
325→    /** The number of whole minutes in this period. Always equal to zero. */
326→    override val minutes: Int get() = 0
327→
328→    /** The number of whole seconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
329→    override val seconds: Int get() = 0
330→
331→    /** The number of nanoseconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
332→    override val nanoseconds: Int get() = 0
333→    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long get() = 0
334→
335→    public companion object {
336→        /**
337→         * Parses the ISO-8601 duration representation as a [DatePeriod].
338→         *
339→         * This function is equivalent to [DateTimePeriod.parse], but will fail if any of the time components are not
340→         * zero.
341→         *
342→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed, the boundaries of [DatePeriod] are exceeded,
343→         * or any time components are not zero.
344→         *
345→         * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
346→         */
347→        public fun parse(text: String): DatePeriod =
348→            when (val period = DateTimePeriod.parse(text)) {
349→                is DatePeriod -> period
350→                else -> throw DateTimeFormatException("Period $period (parsed from string $text) is not date-based")
351→            }
352→    }
353→}
354→
355→/**
356→ * @suppress
357→ */
358→@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DatePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
359→public fun String.toDatePeriod(): DatePeriod = DatePeriod.parse(this)
360→
361→private class DateTimePeriodImpl(
362→    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
363→    override val days: Int,
364→    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long,
365→) : DateTimePeriod()
366→
367→// TODO: these calculations fit in a JS Number. Possible to do an expect/actual here.
368→private fun totalMonths(years: Int, months: Int): Int =
369→    when (val totalMonths = years.toLong() * 12 + months.toLong()) {
370→        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> totalMonths.toInt()
371→        else -> throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of months in $years years and $months months overflows an Int")
372→    }
373→
374→private fun totalNanoseconds(hours: Int, minutes: Int, seconds: Int, nanoseconds: Long): Long {
375→    val totalMinutes: Long = hours.toLong() * 60 + minutes
376→    // absolute value at most 61 * Int.MAX_VALUE
377→    val totalMinutesAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutes * 60
378→    // absolute value at most 61 * 60 * Int.MAX_VALUE < 64 * 64 * 2^31 = 2^43
379→    val minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutesAsSeconds + nanoseconds / NANOS_PER_ONE
380→    // absolute value at most 2^43 + 2^63 / 10^9 < 2^43 + 2^34 < 2^44
381→    val totalSeconds = minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds + seconds
382→    // absolute value at most 2^44 + 2^31 < 2^45
383→    return try {
384→        multiplyAndAdd(totalSeconds, 1_000_000_000, nanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE)
385→    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
386→        throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of nanoseconds in $hours hours, $minutes minutes, $seconds seconds, and $nanoseconds nanoseconds overflows a Long")
387→    }
388→}
389→
390→internal fun buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths: Int = 0, days: Int = 0, totalNanoseconds: Long): DateTimePeriod =
391→    if (totalNanoseconds != 0L)
392→        DateTimePeriodImpl(totalMonths, days, totalNanoseconds)
393→    else
394→        DatePeriod(totalMonths, days)
395→
396→/**
397→ * Constructs a new [DateTimePeriod]. If all the time components are zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
398→ *
399→ * It is recommended to always explicitly name the arguments when constructing this manually,
400→ * like `DateTimePeriod(years = 1, months = 12)`.
401→ *
402→ * The passed numbers are not stored as is but are normalized instead for human readability, so, for example,
403→ * `DateTimePeriod(months = 24)` becomes `DateTimePeriod(years = 2)`.
404→ *
405→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [years] and [months] overflows an [Int].
406→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [hours], [minutes], [seconds] and [nanoseconds]
407→ * overflows a [Long].
408→ */
409→public fun DateTimePeriod(
410→    years: Int = 0,
411→    months: Int = 0,
412→    days: Int = 0,
413→    hours: Int = 0,
414→    minutes: Int = 0,
415→    seconds: Int = 0,
416→    nanoseconds: Long = 0
417→): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths(years, months), days,
418→    totalNanoseconds(hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds))
419→
420→/**
421→ * Constructs a [DateTimePeriod] from a [Duration].
422→ *
423→ * If the duration value is too big to be represented as a [Long] number of nanoseconds,
424→ * the result will be [Long.MAX_VALUE] nanoseconds.
425→ */
426→// TODO: maybe it's more consistent to throw here on overflow?
427→public fun Duration.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalNanoseconds = inWholeNanoseconds)
428→
429→/**
430→ * Adds two [DateTimePeriod] instances.
431→ *
432→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
433→ */
434→public operator fun DateTimePeriod.plus(other: DateTimePeriod): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(
435→    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
436→    safeAdd(days, other.days),
437→    safeAdd(totalNanoseconds, other.totalNanoseconds),
438→)
439→
440→/**
441→ * Adds two [DatePeriod] instances.
442→ *
443→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
444→ */
445→public operator fun DatePeriod.plus(other: DatePeriod): DatePeriod = DatePeriod(
446→    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
447→    safeAdd(days, other.days),
448→)
449→

{"file_path":"/project/core/common/src/DateTimeUnit.kt","old_string":"/**
 * A unit for measuring time.
 *
 * See the predefined constants for time units, like [DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND], [DateTimeUnit.DAY],
 * [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], and others.
 *
 * Two ways are provided to create custom [DateTimeUnit] instances:
 * - By multiplying an existing unit on the right by an integer scalar: for example, `DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND * 10`.
 * - By constructing an instance manually with [TimeBased], [DayBased], or [MonthBased]: for example,
 *   `TimeBased(nanoseconds = 10)`.
 *
 * Note that a calendar day is not considered identical to 24 hours. See [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for a discussion.
 */","new_string":"/**
 * A unit for measuring time.
 *
 * There are two families of units:
 * - [TimeBased] units with a fixed duration (nanoseconds, seconds, hours), suitable for timeline arithmetic.
 * - [DateBased] units (days and months) that depend on the calendar and may require a [TimeZone] when applied to
 *   [Instant] values.
 *
 * See the predefined constants for time units, like [DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND], [DateTimeUnit.DAY],
 * [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], and others.
 *
 * Two ways are provided to create custom [DateTimeUnit] instances:
 * - By multiplying an existing unit on the right by an integer scalar: for example, `DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND * 10`.
 * - By constructing an instance manually with [TimeBased], [DayBased], or [MonthBased]: for example,
 *   `TimeBased(nanoseconds = 10)`.
 *
 * Note that a calendar day is not considered identical to 24 hours. See [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for a discussion.
 *
 * ## Usage
 *
 * - Use [TimeBased] units with [Instant.plus] and [Instant.until] for timeline-based operations.
 * - Use [DateBased] units with [LocalDate.plus]/[LocalDate.until], or with [Instant.plus] in a specific [TimeZone]
 *   for calendar-aware operations.
 *
 * ## Basic example
 * ```kotlin
 * val nextHour = Clock.System.now().plus(1, DateTimeUnit.HOUR)
 * val nextDayInBerlin = Clock.System.now().plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
 * ```
 */"}

 6→package kotlinx.datetime
 7→
 8→import kotlin.time.*
 9→
10→/**
11→ * A source of [Instant] values.
12→ *
13→ * See [Clock.System][Clock.System] for the clock instance that queries the operating system.
14→ */
15→public interface Clock {
16→    /**
17→     * Returns the [Instant] corresponding to the current time, according to this clock.
18→     */
19→    public fun now(): Instant
20→
21→    /**
22→     * The [Clock] instance that queries the operating system as its source of knowledge of time.
23→     */
24→    public object System : Clock {
25→        override fun now(): Instant = @Suppress("DEPRECATION_ERROR") Instant.now()
26→    }
27→
28→    public companion object {
29→
30→    }
31→}
32→
33→/**
34→ * Returns the current date at the given [time zone][timeZone], according to [this Clock][this].
35→ */
36→public fun Clock.todayIn(timeZone: TimeZone): LocalDate =
37→    now().toLocalDateTime(timeZone).date
38→
39→/**
40→ * Returns a [TimeSource] that uses this [Clock] to mark a time instant and to find the amount of time elapsed since that mark.
41→ */
42→@ExperimentalTime
43→public fun Clock.asTimeSource(): TimeSource.WithComparableMarks = object : TimeSource.WithComparableMarks {
44→    override fun markNow(): ComparableTimeMark = InstantTimeMark(now(), this@asTimeSource)
45→}
46→
47→@ExperimentalTime
48→private class InstantTimeMark(private val instant: Instant, private val clock: Clock) : ComparableTimeMark {
49→    override fun elapsedNow(): Duration = saturatingDiff(clock.now(), instant)
50→
51→    override fun plus(duration: Duration): ComparableTimeMark = InstantTimeMark(instant.saturatingAdd(duration), clock)
52→    override fun minus(duration: Duration): ComparableTimeMark = InstantTimeMark(instant.saturatingAdd(-duration), clock)
53→
54→    override fun minus(other: ComparableTimeMark): Duration {
55→        if (other !is InstantTimeMark || other.clock != this.clock) {
56→            throw IllegalArgumentException("Subtracting or comparing time marks from different time sources is not possible: $this and $other")
57→        }
58→        return saturatingDiff(this.instant, other.instant)
59→    }
60→
61→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
62→        return other is InstantTimeMark && this.clock == other.clock && this.instant == other.instant
63→    }
64→
65→    override fun hashCode(): Int = instant.hashCode()
66→
67→    override fun toString(): String = "InstantTimeMark($instant, $clock)"
68→
69→    private fun Instant.isSaturated() = this == Instant.MAX || this == Instant.MIN
70→    private fun Instant.saturatingAdd(duration: Duration): Instant {
71→        if (isSaturated()) {
72→            if (duration.isInfinite() && duration.isPositive() != this.isDistantFuture) {
73→                throw IllegalArgumentException("Summing infinities of different signs")
74→            }
75→            return this
76→        }
77→        return this + duration
78→    }
79→    private fun saturatingDiff(instant1: Instant, instant2: Instant): Duration = when {
80→        instant1 == instant2 ->
81→            Duration.ZERO
82→        instant1.isSaturated() || instant2.isSaturated() ->
83→            (instant1 - instant2) * Double.POSITIVE_INFINITY
84→        else ->
85→            instant1 - instant2
86→    }
87→}
88→
89→@Deprecated("Use Clock.todayIn instead", ReplaceWith("this.todayIn(timeZone)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
90→public fun Clock.todayAt(timeZone: TimeZone): LocalDate = todayIn(timeZone)
91→

 17→ * Instead, its instances can be thought of as clock readings, something that an observer in a particular time zone
 18→ * could witness.

 20→ * this on their clocks at different times.
 21→ *
 22→ * The main purpose of this class is to provide human-readable representations of [Instant] values, or to transfer them
 23→ * as data.
 24→ *
 25→ * The arithmetic on [LocalDateTime] values is not provided, since without accounting for the time zone transitions it may give misleading results.
 26→ */

 37→         * If [format] is not specified, [Formats.ISO] is used.
 38→         *

 42→        public fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<LocalDateTime> = getIsoDateTimeFormat()): LocalDateTime

 44→        /**

 47→         * Examples:

 50→         * LocalDateTime.Format { date(LocalDate.Formats.ISO); char(' '); time(LocalTime.Formats.ISO) }
 51→         *
 52→         * // `08/30 18:43:13`, using a custom format:
 53→         * LocalDateTime.Format {
 54→         *   monthNumber(); char('/'); dayOfMonth()
 55→         *   char(' ')
 56→         *   hour(); char(':'); minute()
 57→         *   optional { char(':'); second() }
 58→         * }
 59→         * ```

 62→         * (for example, [dayOfMonth] is 31 for February), consider using [DateTimeComponents.Format] instead.

 64→         * There is a collection of predefined formats in [LocalDateTime.Formats].

 90→         * - `0000-08-30T18:43:00.5`
 91→         * - `-0001-08-30T18:43:00.123456789`

 97→         */

108→     *          [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] and [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE]

111→     * - [hour] `0..23`
112→     * - [minute] `0..59`
113→     * - [second] `0..59`
114→     * - [nanosecond] `0..999_999_999`
115→     *
116→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any parameter is out of range, or if [dayOfMonth] is invalid for the given [monthNumber] and
117→     * [year].
118→     */
119→    public constructor(
120→        year: Int,
121→        monthNumber: Int,
122→        dayOfMonth: Int,
123→        hour: Int,
124→        minute: Int,
125→        second: Int = 0,
126→        nanosecond: Int = 0

129→    /**

131→     *
132→     * The supported ranges of components:
133→     * - [year] the range is platform dependent, but at least is enough to represent dates of all instants between
134→     *          [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] and [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE]
135→     * - [month] all values of the [Month] enum
